title: Conjunctions and complex sentences
description: Joining short sentences into longer A2 French ones using coordinating and subordinating conjunctions. All explanations in English; French targets with correct accents.

cards.back: because (followed by a full clause with subject + verb)
cards.notes:
**parce que** introduces a reason clause: *Je reste ici **parce qu'**il pleut.* (I am staying here **because** it is raining.) Note the elision before a vowel: *parce que + il* → *parce qu'il*.

**English trap:** *parce que* = because + **clause**. *Pour* = (in order) **to** + infinitive. Never use *pour* when you have a full subject-verb clause.

cards.back: for / because (written register, introduces a reason clause)
cards.notes:
**car** is essentially a more formal, written synonym of *parce que*. It is common in essays and formal writing, less so in speech. Like *parce que*, it is followed by a full clause: *Je reste ici, **car** il pleut.*

Unlike *parce que*, *car* cannot open a sentence — it always comes after the main clause.
cards.hint: car = formal written 'because'; cannot start a sentence.

cards.back: since / given that (explains a reason already known or obvious)
cards.notes:
**puisque** implies the reason is already known or self-evident: ***Puisque** tu es là, on peut commencer.* (Since you are here, we can start.) Elision: *puisque + il* → *puisqu'il*.

Contrast: *parce que* answers *why?* as new information; *puisque* invokes shared knowledge.

When **comme** means 'since / as (because)', it **must** come at the **start** of the sentence: ***Comme** il était fatigué, il est parti tôt.* (As / Since he was tired, he left early.)

Note: *comme* also means 'like / as' in comparisons, but that is a separate use.
cards.hint: Causal comme always opens the sentence.

cards.back: so / therefore (expresses a consequence or conclusion)
cards.notes:
**donc** links a consequence to a cause: *Il pleut, **donc** je reste.* (It is raining, **so** I am staying.) It is very common in both speech and writing.

**Word order:** French keeps normal subject-verb order after *donc* — no inversion, unlike some formal English constructions.

cards.back: then / so (consequence or next step in a sequence)
cards.notes:
**alors** overlaps with *donc* in conversational use: *Tu n'as pas faim? **Alors**, ne mange pas.* (You are not hungry? **Then**, don't eat.) It also marks a time sequence: *Il est arrivé, **alors** on a commencé.* (He arrived, **then** we started.)

*alors* is more colloquial than *donc*.

cards.back: but (contrast or objection between two clauses)
cards.notes:
**mais** is the most basic contrastive conjunction: *J'aime le café **mais** je n'aime pas le thé.* (I like coffee **but** I do not like tea.) It always connects two full clauses or phrases.

This is a direct equivalent of English 'but' with no register restriction.

cards.back: when (introduces a time clause; subject-verb order follows)
cards.notes: **quand** introduces a clause of time: ***Quand** j'arrive, je dis bonjour.* (When I arrive, I say hello.) **Important:** French keeps normal subject-verb order after *quand* — there is no inversion, unlike in German.

cards.back: while (two actions happening at the same time)
cards.notes:
**pendant que** expresses two simultaneous actions: *Je lis **pendant qu'**il cuisine.* (I read **while** he cooks.) Both clauses describe actions in progress at the same time.

Elision: *pendant que + il* → *pendant qu'il*.

cards.back: although (concession — note: requires the subjunctive, a B1 structure)
cards.notes:
**bien que** expresses concession: ***Bien qu'**il soit fatigué, il travaille.* (Although he is tired, he works.) 

At A2, just **recognize** this conjunction and understand its meaning. The subjunctive mood it triggers (*soit* instead of *est*) is a B1 topic. Do not worry about producing it yet.
cards.hint: Recognize bien que = although. The subjunctive after it is B1.

cards.front: ...parce que je suis fatigué / ...quand elle arrive
cards.back: French keeps subject-verb order after all conjunctions
cards.notes:
After every French conjunction — *parce que, quand, pendant que, bien que*, etc. — the clause that follows has **normal subject-verb order**. There is no inversion.

**Contrast with German:** German sends the verb to the end after many conjunctions (*weil ich mude bin*). French does not do this: *parce que je **suis** fatigué* (not *parce que fatigué je suis*).

cards.front: parce qu'il est tard vs. pour rentrer tôt
cards.back: parce que + clause (because) vs. pour + infinitive (in order to)
cards.notes:
A classic English-speaker trap: English 'because' sometimes translates as *pour* when followed by an **infinitive of purpose**, but only *parce que* introduces a full reason clause.

| French | Structure | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| *parce que* | + subject + verb | because (reason) |
| *pour* | + infinitive | (in order) to (purpose) |

*Je pars **parce qu'**il est tard.* (I am leaving because it is late.)
*Je pars **pour** rentrer tôt.* (I am leaving in order to get home early.)

steps.title: Reason conjunctions: parce que, car, puisque, comme
steps.body:
# Conjunctions and complex sentences

A conjunction joins two clauses. French conjunctions **never** change word order in the clause that follows — the subject always precedes the verb.

## Reason conjunctions

| Conjunction | Meaning | Register | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **parce que** | because | all | most common; elision before vowel |
| **car** | for / because | written | cannot open a sentence |
| **puisque** | since / given that | all | reason is already known |
| **comme** | as / since | all | **must** open the sentence |

### Examples

- *Je reste ici **parce qu'**il pleut.* — I am staying here because it is raining.
- *Je reste ici, **car** il pleut.* — I am staying here, for it is raining. (formal)
- ***Puisque** tu es là, on peut commencer.* — Since you are here, we can start.
- ***Comme** il était fatigué, il est parti tôt.* — As he was tired, he left early.

## English trap: parce que vs. pour

English 'because' can sometimes feel like a purpose, but French is strict:

- **parce que** + full clause (subject + verb) → expresses a **reason**
- **pour** + infinitive → expresses a **purpose** (in order to)

*Je travaille **parce que** j'ai besoin d'argent.* (because I need money — reason)
*Je travaille **pour** gagner de l'argent.* (in order to earn money — purpose)

steps.title: Consequence and contrast: donc, alors, mais
steps.body:
## Consequence conjunctions

| Conjunction | Meaning | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| **donc** | so / therefore | conclusion or logical consequence |
| **alors** | then / so | consequence or time sequence; more colloquial |

- *Il pleut, **donc** je reste.* — It is raining, so I am staying.
- *Tu n'as pas faim ? **Alors**, ne mange pas.* — You are not hungry? Then don't eat.

*donc* is slightly more formal; *alors* is everyday speech.

## Contrast

| Conjunction | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **mais** | but |

- *J'aime le café **mais** je n'aime pas le thé.* — I like coffee but I do not like tea.

## Word order reminder

After **all** French conjunctions, the following clause uses **normal subject-verb order** — no inversion, no verb-at-end (unlike German). This applies to every conjunction in this lesson.

steps.title: Time conjunctions and bien que
steps.body:
## Time conjunctions

| Conjunction | Meaning | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **quand** | when | same time or sequence |
| **pendant que** | while | two simultaneous ongoing actions |

- ***Quand** j'arrive, je dis bonjour.* — When I arrive, I say hello.
- *Je lis **pendant qu'**il cuisine.* — I read while he cooks.

Both use elision before a vowel: *quand + il* → *quand il* (no elision needed here), *pendant que + il* → *pendant qu'il*.

## bien que — recognition only at A2

**bien que** means *although / even though* and expresses concession. It is followed by the **subjonctif**, which is a B1 grammar topic.

- ***Bien qu'**il soit fatigué, il travaille.* — Although he is tired, he works.

At A2 you only need to **recognise** *bien que* and understand that it means *although*. The subjunctive form it requires (*soit* instead of *est*) will be studied at B1.

## Summary table — all conjunctions in this lesson

| Conjunction | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| parce que | because | reason |
| car | for / because | reason (written) |
| puisque | since / given that | reason (shared knowledge) |
| comme | as / since | reason (sentence-initial) |
| donc | so / therefore | consequence |
| alors | then / so | consequence / sequence |
| mais | but | contrast |
| quand | when | time |
| pendant que | while | time (simultaneous) |
| bien que | although | concession (+ subjonctif, B1) |

exercise.prompt: Match each French conjunction to its English meaning.
Match each conjunction to its near-equivalent.
Translate: *I am staying home because I am tired.* (Use parce que.)
accept: Je reste à la maison parce que je suis fatigué.
Je reste à la maison parce que je suis fatiguée.
je reste à la maison parce que je suis fatigué
Je reste chez moi parce que je suis fatigué.
Je reste chez moi parce que je suis fatiguée.
exercise.hint: parce que is followed by a full clause: parce que + subject + verb.
distractors: Je reste à la maison parce je suis fatigué.
Je reste à la maison pour être fatigué.
Je reste à la maison because que je suis fatigué.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *It is late, so we are leaving.* (Use donc.)
accept: Il est tard, donc nous partons.
il est tard, donc nous partons
Il est tard donc nous partons.
Il est tard, donc on part.
exercise.hint: donc comes after the first clause, then normal subject-verb order follows.
distractors: Il est tard, donc partons nous.
Il est tard, alors que nous partons.
Il est tard, pour nous partons.

exercise.prompt: Complete the sentence with the correct conjunction meaning 'because'.
exercise.sentence: Elle ne vient pas ___ elle est malade.

accept: parce que
parce qu'
car
puisque

exercise.hint: parce que is the most common way to say 'because' in French.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the blank with the French word for 'when'.
exercise.sentence: ___ il fait beau, nous allons au parc.

accept: Quand
quand

exercise.hint: The clause that follows uses normal subject-verb order.

steps.title: Build a sentence with pendant que

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to say: *I read while she watches TV.*
exercise.hint: pendant que introduces the simultaneous action; elision before a vowel gives qu'elle.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to say: *He likes coffee but he does not like tea.*
exercise.hint: mais connects the two full clauses; subject-verb order stays normal on both sides.

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly translates: *She is leaving because she is tired.*

images.label: Elle part parce qu'elle est fatiguée.
Elle part pour elle est fatiguée.
Elle part because qu'elle est fatiguée.

exercise.hint: parce que introduces a full clause with subject + verb. pour is for infinitives.
distractors: Elle part pour elle est fatiguée.
Elle part because qu'elle est fatiguée.

exercise.prompt: What does this sentence mean in English? *Bien qu'il soit fatigué, il continue de travailler.*
accept: Although he is tired, he continues to work.
Although he is tired, he keeps working.
Even though he is tired, he continues to work.
Even though he is tired, he keeps working.
Although he is tired he continues to work.
exercise.hint: bien que = although / even though. The verb form 'soit' (subjunctive) is a B1 topic — just focus on the meaning.
distractors: Because he is tired, he continues to work.
While he is tired, he continues to work.
So he is tired, he continues to work.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *As the shop was closed, we went home.* (Use comme; it must open the sentence.)
accept: Comme le magasin était fermé, nous sommes rentrés.
Comme le magasin était fermé, nous sommes rentrées.
Comme le magasin était fermé, on est rentré.
Comme la boutique était fermée, nous sommes rentrés.
exercise.hint: comme must come first; the main clause follows with normal subject-verb order.
distractors: Nous sommes rentrés comme le magasin était fermé.
Parce comme le magasin était fermé, nous sommes rentrés.
Comme le magasin était fermé alors nous sommes rentrés comme.
